coredata.dc:title: Novel Ce@N-CDs as green corrosion inhibitor for metal in acidic environment
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhixiong
Hao, Xiaoyu
Li, Y.
Zhang, Xianhui
coredata.dc:description: Ce and N elements were selected as dopants for modifying the surface of carbon dots to obtain a novel Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. The structure and inhibition ability of the as-obtained Ce@N-CDs were analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and electrochemical workstation. The results showed that the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor exhibited good dispersion in solution with average particle size of about 3 ∼ 6 nm. Electrochemical measurements indicated that the corrosion resistance of metal (Q235 steel) in aggressive solution (1 M HCl) was greatly enhanced after adding Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. The inhibition efficiency was up to 96.4% when the addition amount of Ce@N-CDs was 200 mg/L. This inhibition effect was mainly due to the physicochemical adsorption and the formation of Ce-containing ligand complexes on corrosion interface, which was confirmed by the XPS analysis of corrosion product.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ce@N-CDs
Electrochemical behavior
Physicochemical adsorption
Corrosion product

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The detailed synthetic scheme of Ce@N-CDs inhibitor.
Structural characterization of the nanoparticles: (a) FTIR (b) XPS (c) Ce 3d (d) N 1 s (e) C 1 s (f) TEM.
Bode plots and Nyquist plots for samples after exposure to the solution.
Equivalent circuit model for fitting the EIS results.
Tafel curves for the blank sample and samples with the inhibitor.
SEM micrographs of the carbon steel surface after immersion.
LSEM micrographs of the carbon steel surface after immersion.
Corrosion rate of metal after various immersion time in 1 M HCl solution without and with Ce@N-CDs inhibitor.
Ce 3d (a) and N 1 s (b) spectra of metal surface after immersion.
Schematic representation of the protective mechanism of Ce@N-CDs inhibitor.
EIS data for steel after immersion.
Tafel data for steel in 1 M HCl without and with the inhibitor

head.ce:title: Novel Ce@N-CDs as green corrosion inhibitor for metal in acidic environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400045, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400045, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian province key laboratory of naval architecture and marine engineering, School of Marine Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian province key laboratory of naval architecture and marine engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian province key laboratory of naval architecture and marine engineering, School of Marine Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ce and N elements were selected as dopants for modifying the surface of carbon dots to obtain a novel Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. The structure and inhibition ability of the as-obtained Ce@N-CDs were analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and electrochemical workstation. The results showed that the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor exhibited good dispersion in solution with average particle size of about 3 ∼ 6 nm. Electrochemical measurements indicated that the corrosion resistance of metal (Q235 steel) in aggressive solution (1 M HCl) was greatly enhanced after adding Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. The inhibition efficiency was up to 96.4% when the addition amount of Ce@N-CDs was 200 mg/L. This inhibition effect was mainly due to the physicochemical adsorption and the formation of Ce-containing ligand complexes on corrosion interface, which was confirmed by the XPS analysis of corrosion product.

ce:section.ce:para: Fracturing acidification is currently the mainstream method used for oil and gas field stimulation. In the acid fracturing process, the injection of liquid acid can largely relieve blockages in the oil and gas wellbore and formation and increase the permeability of the matrix, thus enhancing the oil and gas recovery rate [1,2]. However, acidizing construction also brings many problems to the oilfield. During the acidizing construction process, the injection of liquid acids such as hydrochloric acid will cause corrosion of pipe strings, construction pipelines and metal equipment in oil and gas wells. In severe cases, it may cause sudden rupture of downhole pipes and perforation of pipelines, posing serious safety hazards. At the same time, the metal ions dissolved by acid may cause damage to the formation. Adding a corrosion inhibitor to the acid is the most commonly used and effective anti-corrosion measure to prevent the acid from corroding the tubing, casing and construction equipment [3,4]. At present, most of the commercially available corrosion inhibitors have shortcomings, such as easy coking, stratification, unstable dissolution and dispersion performance, toxic raw materials, flammability and explosive pollution at high temperatures [5]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a new type of acidizing corrosion inhibitor with simple formula and mild synthesis conditions, which is also non-toxic, environmentally friendly, and capable of meeting the harsh requirements of current oil and gas well acidizing construction [6-17].

In recent years, carbon dots and rare-earth inhibitors have received extensive attention due to their excellent physical and chemical properties and many potential applications [18-23]. At present, carbon dots have set off a research boom in the field of corrosion engineering due to their good water dispersion, chemical inertness, corrosion resistance and biocompatibility [24-26]. Ye et al. [27-29] designed a series of N-CDs from natural citric acid based material, which exhibited inhibition efficiency of more than 90%, and mixed-type inhibition mechanism. Lv et al. [30] synthesized new N-CDs based on low-cost materials such urotropine, citric acid, and diethylenetriamine by microwave synthesis methodology, which were suitable as economic, efficient corrosion inhibitor for the gas and oil industry. Cen et al. [31,32] first investigated the inhibition performance of new N, S-CDs for metals in aggressive acidic medium. The adsorption behaviors of these N, S-CDs followed the Langmuir isotherm and the obtained Gibbs free energies confirmed their physicochemical adsorption on steel surface. Li et al. [33] selected the rare earth salt of Ce(SO4)2 as a good corrosion inhibitor in 0.5 M citric acid solution and found that it effectively improved the surface morphology of steel. Yong et al. [34] studied the inhibitory mechanism of steel in 3.5% NaCl solution containing a Ce3+-based inhibitor. The obtained results confirmed that Ce element played a great role in corrosion protection.

However, at present, there is little research on the collaborative protection of Ce and carbon dots in aggressive solution. Thus, this work deeply analyzes the cooperative protection behavior and mechanism of Ce and carbon dots in corrosion inhibition.

Cerium nitrate hexahydrate and citric acid were obtained from Universal Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrothermal synthesis reactor, constant temperature dialysis belt (with flattened width of 45 mm and molecular weight of 1000), thermostatic needle filters and centrifuge tubes were purchased from China Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. Ultrasonic cleaner equipment was procured from Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd. RE-52A constant temperature rotary electric evaporator was obtained from Shanghai Yarong Biochemical Instrument Factory. X 85–2 constant temperature magnetic stirrer was purchased from Shanghai Meiyingpu Co., Ltd. Constant temperature electric heating blast drying oven was obtained from Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd. Q235 mild steel with size of 1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm was purchased from Shengxin technology Co., Ltd., and its composition is listed in Table S1.

The preparation process of novel inhibitor is displayed in Fig. 1 . First, 1.68 g of citric acid and 0.4 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved in a beaker containing 50 mL ultrapure water. After that, the solution was transferred to an ultrasonic cleaner to accelerate the dissolution and form a uniform solution. Finally, the solution was placed in a reactor and heated in a blast electric drying oven at 200 °C for 6 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature, and the black impurities was removed by filtration. During dialysis, the inside and outside of the dialysis bag were rinsed with ultrapure water three times. Before filling the fluid, the dialysis bag was tested for leaks. In order to prevent the dialysis bag from being ruptured, a certain space was left after filling the fluid. A small magnetic stirrer was used for mixing and the dialysis process was performed in a large beaker filled with distilled water for 6 h. The filtered solution was placed in a centrifuge tube and then freeze-dried in the refrigerator. The obtained Ce-doped carbon dots (Ce@N-CDs) were light brownish green.

The structure and composition were analyzed by FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, XPS and TEM. A CHI660e electrochemical workstation was employed to perform the electrochemical behavior analysis of sample, and four sets of experiments were carried out for the electrochemical measurements. The obtained electrochemical data were fitted using CHI660e software. In the electrochemical tests, a typical three–electrode system was employed consisting of a reference electrode of saturated calomel, counter electrode of platinum and working electrode of Q235 steel. The working electrode was immersed in 1 M HCl without and with the Ce@N-CDs corrosion inhibitor for 24 h. During the immersion, the potential of the electrode surface reached a steady state.

ZSimDemo 3.30d software was employed to collect and analyze the EIS data over the frequency range of 100 kHz ∼ 0.01 Hz with AC voltage amplitude of 10 mV. The Tafel curves were obtained in the potential range of −0.25 to 0.25 V versus SCE with a scanning rate of 1 mV/s. The surface coverage (θ) and inhibition efficiency (IE) of the Ce@N-CDs corrosion inhibitor was calculated according to Eqs. (1) and (2) [5,35]:
(1)
θ =
i
c o r r
0
-
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
0
(2)
I E =
i
c o r r
0
-
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
0
× 100 %

where i0 corr and icorr are the corrosion current densities in the corrosive and inhibited 1 M HCl solution, respectively. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) analyses were performed in inhibited and corrosive 1 M HCl solution. Moreover, the corrosion rate of steel in testing medium was determined according to the National Association of Corrosion Engineers Standard (NACE TM0169/G31) [36]. The metal sample was immersed in the studied solutions for different time (2, 6, 12, 24 h) before the experiment. Finally, XPS detection was used to analyze the corrosion product on the steel surface.

The structural characterization results of Ce@N-CDs are presented in Fig. 2 . As seen from the FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2a, some characteristic peaks were observed at 1296.1, 1436.9, 1538.1, 1647.3, 1693.9, 2836.2, and 3402.3 cm−1, which were assigned to the vibration of C-O, C-N, C = N, –CO-NH-, C = O, C-H, and N-H bonds [37]. Importantly, the appearance of C-N, C = N and –CO-NH- peaks confirmed that the N element was successfully doped into the carbon dots. The XPS results are shown in Fig. 2 b ∼ e. Several sharp peaks were detected at 296, 400, 531, 889 and 905 eV, which corresponded to C, N, O, and Ce elements, suggesting the successful doping of Ce. To verify the valence state of Ce element, the XPS analysis results are shown in Fig. 2 c. In XPS-Ce 3d spectrum, the four peaks at 884.9, 903.2, 890 and 908.2 eV were assigned to Ce3+ and Ce4+, based on a previous study [38]. In N 1 s spectrum, two peaks were located at 399 and 401 eV [39], which illustrated that the bonding forms of N element were N-H and N-C (Fig. 2 d). Similarly, in the C 1 s spectrum of Ce@N-CDs shown in Fig. 2 e, the peaks at 284.8, 285.5, 287.4, and 287.9 eV were assigned to C-C, C-N, O = C-N, and C = O bonds, respectively [40]. As seen from the TEM result in Fig. 2 f, Ce@N-CDs were well-dispersed, the particle shape was granular and the average size was about 3 ∼ 6 nm.

The variation of open circuit potential (OCP) value vs time is shown in Fig. S1 . The results show that, as time progressed, the OCP value began to fluctuate to some extent, and then reached a stable state. The measured OCP value was −0.47 ± 0.01 V vs SCE when the Ce@N-CDs concentration was 50 mg/L. With the increase in Ce@N-CDs concentration, the OCP value in the stable state showed a slight drop and reached −0.53 ± 0.02 V vs SCE at 200 mg/L.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used to monitor the anti-corrosion behavior of Ce@N-CDs during immersion in the acidic solution under the inhibited condition. Fig. 3 shows the Nyquist and Bode plots after immersion in corrosive and inhibited solutions. Due to the frequency dispersion and roughness of steel surface, the semicircle did not have an ideal shape [41]. The radius of capacitive reactance arc in the Nyquist image and |Z |f =0.01Hz in the Bode image revealed that the addition of Ce@N-CDs enhanced the impedance of metal to different degrees. With the increase in Ce@N-CDs additive amount, the radius of capacitive reactance arc was enlarged, suggesting the improved protective effect [42]. This behavior indicated that the corrosion resistance of steel was proportional to the concentration of inhibitor due to the increase in surface coverage. More inhibitor molecules covered the extensive surface area of steel at higher concentrations, thereby preventing the corrosion reaction to a large extent. In the Bode plot, the |Z |f =0.01Hz value in blank solution was about 10 Ω cm2. After adding 50 and 100 mg/L of Ce@N-CDs, the |Z |f =0.01Hz was enhanced by seven and nine times, respectively. However, the |Z|f=0.01Hz value showed an order of magnitude change with further increase in additive amount. The Bode plot in Fig. 3 c displays the phase angles in a wide frequency range. Clearly, the phase angle was the smallest in the corrosive solution. As the additive amount increased, the phase angle and its range both increased, implying that the adsorption of Ce@N-CDs occurred on the metal surface, which provided effective protection to the metal.

In order to study the influence of immersion time on the corrosion inhibition behavior of Ce@N-CDs, the group with a concentration of 200 mg/L was selected as the research object. The immersion time was 2, 6, 12 and 24 h. The radius of capacitive reactance arcs followed the order of 2 h < 6 h < 12 h < 24 h. This indicated that Ce@N-CDs displayed a stronger protective effect under long-term adsorption and the thickness of the adsorption protective layer was increased. In order to better illustrate the change of impedance, the |Z|f =0.01Hz values under different immersion time were calculated. Through analysis, the |Z|f =0.01Hz value after 2 h immersion was about 76 Ω cm2. Its value increased from 100 to 1780 Ω cm2 as the immersion time increased from 6 to 24 h. In addition, the effect of immersion time on phase angle was similar to that of Ce@N-CDs concentration.

The EIS data were fitted by an equivalent electrical circuit to acquire more information about the electrochemical process during the immersion. The fitting results are shown in Fig. 4 . Here, Rs represents the solution resistance, while the polarization resistance and constant phase element are given by Rp (=Rf + Rct) and Cdl, respectively [43,44]. The calculated results of these parameters are listed in Table 1 . Since the main components of the solution were the same, the resistance of the solution was similar. With the increase in Ce@N-CDs additive amount, Rp and n exhibited an uptrend, which reflected that the difficulty of the interface reaction increased and the interface roughness decreased. Specifically, Rp and n increased from 71.6 Ω cm2 and 0.95 to 1804.3 Ω cm2 and 0.98 when the Ce@N-CDs additive amount increased, respectively. However, Cdl showed an opposite trend to n, which decreased from 68.5 to 11.6 μF cm−2. With the decrease in immersion time, Rp and n exhibited a downtrend and Cdl revealed an increasing trend. The increase in thickness of the adsorption protective layer caused the change in these parameter values [45]. These phenomena further illustrated that Ce@N-CDs was a high-efficiency and continuous inhibitor, which could effectively inhibit the corrosion of metals in acidic conditions, especially at high concentrations and long immersion time.

Fig. 5 shows the Tafel curves of metal in 1 M HCl without and with the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. Some important Tafel parameters, such as corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current density (icorr), Tafel anodic (βa) and cathodic (βc) slopes, θ and IE, were estimated from the Tafel curves using professional analysis software, which are listed in Table 2 . In general, corrosion potential can be used to distinguish the mechanism type of corrosion inhibitor. If Ecorr is higher than 0.085 V, it indicates that the inhibitory activity of inhibitor could be cathodic or anode type. If Ecorr is lower than 0.085 V, it indicates that the inhibitor is a mixed type [41]. The results showed that the Ecorr value was lower than 0.085 V under all conditions, revealing that the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor was a mixed-type inhibitor. The cathodic and anodic Tafel curves were shifted to lower icorr regions after adding Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. Moreover, the extent of the shift greatly depended on the increase in Ce@N-CDs concentration. For instance, a higher Ce@N-CDs concentration led to a lower icorr value. These phenomena verified that the electrochemical reactions of cathode and anode induced by Ce@N-CDs in 1 M HCl solution exhibited a significant anti-corrosion effect on the metal surface. The increase in concentration of Ce@N-CDs improved the corrosion resistance of Ce@N-CDs.

It can be seen from Table 2 that icorr was higher in blank solution than in the inhibited medium. Moreover, the increase in Ce@N-CDs concentration sharply reduced the value of icorr. These facts implied that the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor was readily adsorbed on the metal surface by its electron-rich centers, and the fraction of blocked active sites on steel/solution interface increased with the increase in inhibitor concentration. Thus, the number of active corrosion zones on the metal surface decreased. At the same time, analyzing the data from Table 2 indicated that βa and βc values were dramatically changed due to the great influence of Ce@N-CDs inhibitor on anodic and cathodic corrosion. This was because Ce@N-CDs was a mixed inhibitor. Hence, it could reduce the electron density at the anode and cathode sites on the metal surface [41]. As the Ce@N-CDs additive amount increased, IE increased from 87.8% at 50 mg/L to 96.4% at 200 mg/L. The increase in IE value with concentration was due to the adsorption of more inhibitor molecules on steel/solution interface. Moreover, compared with other carbon dots-based inhibitors in Table S2, Ce@N-CDs exhibited a stronger protection ability for metal during corrosion process [18,24-29,42,46-48].

Surface corrosion morphology was observed after different immersion time in 1 M HCl solution without and with Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. The SEM images of the corroded metal are presented in Fig. 6 . In blank solution, many corroded regions were observed on the metal surface. Since this aggressive environment was highly corrosive and easily corroded the metal surface, the corrosion products were deposited on the metal surface. By comparison, the quality of the metal surface was greatly enhanced in the presence of Ce@N-CDs inhibitor because the metal surface was protected by the adsorption of Ce@N-CDs. The protection effect was proportional to the additive amount of Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. Moreover, the immersion time also had a great influence on the corrosion morphology. The corrosion of metal surface became less obvious and the defects were significantly reduced with immersion time.

Results of 3D surface corrosion morphology and interface roughness (Ra) were obtained after various immersion time in 1 M HCl solution without and with Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. Fig. 7 displays the 3D micrographs of the corroded metal. In blank solution, some deep pits and high bumps were found on the metal surface, which may be caused by the aggregated corrosive effect of high concentrations of corrosive ions. The measured Ra value in blank solution was 1.018 μm. After adding Ce@N-CDs inhibitor, both the number of deep pits and the height of the bumps were reduced. Thus, Ra was greatly reduced. For example, the Ra values were 0.731, 0.452, 0.371 and 0.258 μm at 50, 100, 150 and 200 mg/L of Ce@N-CDs after 24 h immersion. Compared with blank solution, these values were reduced by 28.2%, 55.6%, 63.6% and 74.7%, respectively. At the same time, Ra was obviously affected by immersion time because the corrosion reaction took time to complete. The measured Ra values were 0.493, 0.386 and 0.314 μm at 200 mg/L of Ce@N-CDs after 2, 6 and 12 h immersion. Along with the immersion time, Ra of the interface surface revealed a downtrend, as the number of deep pits and bumps were gradually reduced.

The corrosion rate of metal substrate after various immersion time in 1 M HCl solution without and with Ce@N-CDs inhibitor can be evaluated by weight loss tests. The results are summarized in Fig. 8 . The average corrosion rate of metal in blank solution was about 2.2 × 10-3 g/cm·h. After adding 50 mg/L of Ce@N-CDs, the value was reduced to 9.1 × 10-4 g/cm·h, which was decreased by 58.6% in comparison to blank solution. These results indicated that Ce@N-CDs provided better resistance against the damage from corrosive condition. This can be ascribed to the good dispersion of Ce@N-CDs in the solution, which helped to reduce the attack of aggressive ions, resulting in the better protection effect for metal. Furthermore, the corrosion rate was inversely proportional to the additive amount of Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. The immersion time also had a great influence on the corrosion rate. As the immersion time increased, the corrosion rate in blank solution revealed an uptrend, while the change trend was reversed after adding Ce@N-CDs.

XPS analysis was performed to identify the corrosion product of metal in 1 M HCl solution without and with Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. Fig. 9 shows the Ce 3d and N 1 s spectra of metal surface after immersion. As seen in Fig. 9 a, the fitted peaks at 898.3, 903.2, 916.6 and 920.3 eV were assigned to CeO2, Ce2O3, CeO2 and CeN, respectively [49], implying that Ce formed oxygen-containing ligand complexes and nitrogen-containing ligand complexes with oxygen ions and nitrogen ions. Fig. 9 b exhibits the fitting spectrum of N 1 s from the corrosion product, in which the peaks at 396.7, 399.5, and 401.4 eV were assigned to N-Ce, N-C, and N-Fe bonds, respectively [50,51]. This suggested that N could bond with Fe ions on the metal surface to prevent the aggressive ion.

The adsorption mechanism of Ce@N-CDs molecule can be illustrated by adsorption isotherms. In this work, the Langmuir model was applied to evaluate the adsorption performance of the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor on metal, and the corresponding isotherm was obtained from Eqs. (3) and (4) [52,53]:
(3)
θ
1 - θ
=
K
a d s
C
(4)
Δ
G
a d s
0
= - R T I n
( 1000
K
a d s
)

where θ is the surface coverage, C is the inhibitor concentration, Kads is the adsorption equilibrium constant, R is 8.314 J/mol-1K−1 and T is 298 K. The results show that the value of R2 was close to 1 for the Langmuir isotherm, confirming the high fitting accuracy of the adsorption model. The equilibrium constant value of adsorption–desorption processes (Kads) is the reciprocal of the intercept in the Langmuir isotherm. The calculated result of Kads is presented in Fig. 10 . According to a previous study [54], the value of ΔG0 ads ≥ − 20 kJ mol−1 corresponds to the physical adsorption process, which is because of the electrostatic interaction between the inhibitor and the negatively charged metal surface. The value of ΔG0 ads ≤ -40 kJ mol−1 corresponds to the chemical adsorption processes, which mainly involves the sharing of electron pairs from heteroatoms or π electrons from aromatic rings to form rigid coordination bonds. In this work, the ΔG0 ads value was between 20 ∼ 40 kJ mol−1, confirming the physicochemical adsorption of the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor on metal in 1 M HCl solution. This indicated that Ce@N-CDs was strongly adsorbed on the metal surface through electrostatic and chemical interactions.

The possible corrosion protection mechanism of Ce@N-CDs is proposed in Fig. 11 . In general, if the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor was not added in the solution, the metal surface was corroded by aggressive ions such as H+ and Cl-. After adding Ce@N-CDs inhibitor, the protective effect came from three aspects. First, Ce@N-CDs inhibitor molecules were adsorbed on the metal surface by electrostatic action, and then covered the metal surface. Although this effect was weak, it could also inhibit the contact of corrosive ions to a certain extent. Second, the electron pairs in the d–orbitals of iron were reallocated to unoccupied orbitals of the Ce@N-CDs molecules. This behavior strengthened the bond between the corrosion inhibitor and the metal interface, which could not be easily separated by external forces. Finally, Ce ion could form oxygen-containing ligand complexes and nitrogen-containing ligand complexes with oxygen ions and nitrogen ions. In this work, carbon dots served as the nitrogen-containing ligand and oxygen was also present in the corrosive media. Ce ion has strong coordination ability with oxygen and nitrogen ligands. Hence, it is very likely to form an oxygen-containing or nitrogen-containing ligand complex. Therefore, it is speculated that such a complex may be formed in the solution and adsorbed on the surface of substrate.

In this work, a novel Ce doped carbon dots anti-corrosive inhibitor was prepared to protect mild steel from corrosion damage. The structural characterization suggested that Ce and N were successfully doped into carbon dots, and the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor revealed a uniform distribution with particle size of 3 ∼ 6 nm. Compared with blank solution, the electrochemical resistance was dramatically enhanced and the current density was greatly reduced in the presence of the inhibitor, implying that the Ce@N-CDs inhibitor provided excellent protection for the metal sample in corrosion medium. This protective effect was attributed to the physical adsorption caused by electrostatic interaction, the chemical adsorption caused by unsaturated bond pairing, and the formation of Ce-containing complexes. Therefore, with its merits of high corrosion inhibition efficiency and low cost, Ce@N-CDs inhibitor is a very promising alternative to traditional inhibitors and could significantly extend the service life of metals in corrosive environments.

This article was completed with the joint efforts of several authors. In detail, Zhixiong Liu is responsible for Writing - original draft and Funding acquisition; Xiaoyu Hao and Y. Li are responsible for Data curation and Writing - review & editing; Xianhui Zhang is responsible for Supervision, Validation and Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been encouraged and supported by Doctor Research Fund of Shanxi Datong University (Grant No. 2019-B-01); Science Foundation of Shanxi Datong University (2020YGZX011); Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2019L0739); Innovation & Application Engineering Research Center for Mesoporous Materials of Shanxi Province (MMIA2019101); Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, CAS (2021 K02).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.118155.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

J. Mol Liq.
